frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment: Rajan: Suno, Priya ka phone aaya tha. Bol rahi hai surname nahi badlegi shaadi ke baad. Apna hi naam rakhegi — professionally, personally, sab mein. Maine bol diya theek hai, but theek hai kahan? Yeh ladka keh raha hai usse koi problem nahi — but koi bhi mard sach mein yeh sunna chahta hai kya? Andar se toh lagega hi na ki biwi usse apna maanna nahi chahti. Surname toh ek symbol hota hai na — ki tum ek ho gaye, ki ek nayi family bani. Log kya sochenge? Sharma ji waghera poochenge, log samjhenge ki koi takleef hai ghar mein. Aur yeh ek cheez hoti hai — ek cheez se dusri baat niklegi. Aaj naam nahi badla, kal kuch aur. Maine soch ke pala hai usse, acchi life dene ke liye — yeh sab 'modern' hone ki zaroorat kya hai ab?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Rajan frames his daughter's autonomous decision about her own name as a threat to marital unity and a source of social shame, implying that a woman's identity must be absorbed into her husband's as a symbol of true marriage. The concern about 'what people will think' and the slippery-slope reasoning ('aaj naam, kal kuch aur') encode the misogynistic assumption that a wife's individuality signals disloyalty or an incomplete surrender to marriage.
persona_text: You are Rajan. Age 55. Married. A retired government officer in Lucknow. Your daughter Priya who is 28 and lives in Delhi just called to tell you she has decided not to change her surname after her upcoming marriage and will keep her name professionally and personally. She says her husband to be is fine with it. You said okay on the phone. But you are not okay. You are now talking to your wife in the bedroom after the call. You are telling her what Priya said and what you think it means and what people will say and whether her husband to be is really fine with it or just saying so and what a woman who does not take her husband's name is signalling about how she sees her marriage.
